comments: This manuscript is a short expository note that summarizes three Bayesian-style bandit algorithms: Information-Directed Sampling (IDS), Thompson Sampling (TS) and Generalized Thompson Sampling (GTS). The author attempts to give intuitive explanations for each algorithm, fill in derivations omitted from the original papers (in particular for the Bernoulli bandit case), and sketch regret analyses. Strengths: - The note collects material about three related algorithms in one place and walks through concrete derivations (e.g., αt for Beta posteriors, conditional expectations used in IDS, and the outline of the TS regret decomposition). This can be useful as a learning aid for readers who want additional detail beyond the original papers. - The author points out practical issues (notably the computational burden of IDS integrals) which practitioners should be aware of. Weaknesses and concerns: - Presentation and formatting: the manuscript contains many typographical and formatting problems (repeated paragraphs, stray LaTeX tokens, inconsistent notation such as spacing in subscripts/superscripts, and references to figures that are not included). These issues reduce readability and make some equations hard to follow. - Repetition and organization: portions of the introduction and problem statements are duplicated. The flow could be improved by removing redundancy and grouping related material more tightly. - Technical completeness: some derivations are presented at a high level or in a sketchy way. Important steps are asserted without adequate justification (for example, several uses of conditioning and equalities in the IDS derivations are plausible but not fully justified in the text). The regret bounds are stated and sketched but the note frequently refers back to the original papers for important details rather than providing self-contained proofs. - Empirical validation: the note comments that IDS is computationally heavy but provides no empirical experiments or timing/complexity analysis to support or quantify that claim. For a note that emphasizes omitted derivations, it would strengthen the contribution to include at least a small numerical experiment (or pseudo-code complexity analysis) to illustrate the computational cost and the accuracy of the numerical integration approximations used. - Novelty: the manuscript is largely expository and does not appear to contain new theoretical results. That is acceptable for a tutorial/note, but the intended contribution should be clearly framed as exposition/derivation rather than new algorithmic advances. Suggestions for improvement: 1) Carefully proofread and clean the manuscript: remove duplicated text, fix LaTeX/formatting artifacts, ensure consistent notation, and include any referenced figures. 2) Make the derivations more self-contained where possible, or explicitly state which steps are omitted and give precise pointers to the original results. 3) If claiming computational drawbacks of IDS, include a small empirical section showing runtimes and error of numerical integration for moderate K, or provide complexity analysis and practical approximations (e.g., Monte Carlo, quadrature choices). 4) Clarify the contribution and target audience in the introduction: is this a tutorial for students, a set of worked derivations for researchers, or a short survey? 5) Improve the discussion comparing the three methods — both theoretically (where they differ in posterior updates and regret guarantees) and practically (computational trade-offs). Overall recommendation: the note is a useful pedagogical write-up that requires significant polishing before broad distribution. It is suitable for arXiv as an expository note after revision, but in its current form it needs improvements in clarity, organization, and completeness.
